title: Repairing a chipped laminate worktop
subtitle: Fill and colour-match a chip or edge damage on a kitchen worktop
excerpt: How to repair a chip or edge damage on a laminate kitchen worktop: cleaning the damaged area, filling with a colour-matched filler, levelling, and finishing to reduce the visibility of the repair.

glossaryTerms.definition: A kitchen worktop made from a particleboard core surfaced with a thin layer of decorative high-pressure laminate (HPL), typically 0.7 to 1.0 mm thick. The laminate surface is hard and heat-resistant but brittle at exposed edges; impacts cause chips that expose the brown particleboard core and can allow water to penetrate and swell the board.

The rounded front edge profile on a laminate worktop, formed by bending the laminate sheet over the curved particleboard edge during manufacture under heat and pressure. Post-formed edges are the most common UK kitchen worktop profile and are the edges most vulnerable to chipping at corners.

content.text: , exposes the particleboard core. The repair will not be invisible on close inspection but it stops water ingress and reduces the visual impact of the damage significantly. Colour-matched wax sticks give the closest result; laminate repair paste in a tube is an alternative.
Step 1, clean and dry the damage.

Remove any loose chips or particles from the damaged area. Clean with a dry cloth to remove dust. If the particleboard core is already swollen, allow the area to dry completely before filling; swollen particleboard must be fully dry before it can accept a filler.
Step 2, select and apply the wax stick.

Choose a wax stick from the repair set that matches the worktop colour as closely as possible in natural daylight. Warm the tip of the stick with a hair dryer or a brief touch from a heat gun on low setting until the wax is soft. Work the wax into the chip with the filling knife, slightly overfilling the damage so the wax stands above the surface level.

Allow the wax to cool and harden for five minutes. Hold the filling knife flat against the surface and shave the proud wax flush with a single firm draw across the repair. Repeat until the fill is level with the surrounding laminate. Do not use sandpaper on a wax fill; it scratches the laminate and smears the wax.

Buff the repair with a clean dry cloth. If the worktop has a gloss finish, apply a small amount of the appropriate worktop sealant over the repair and the surrounding 100 mm to restore the sheen. A matte finish needs no additional treatment.
Attempting the repair with the worktop wet.
Wax will not adhere to a damp or recently washed surface. The repair area must be bone dry.
Colour too dark or too light.

Test the wax on a hidden area of the worktop edge before filling the visible chip. Mix two wax stick colours together if no single colour matches; soften both on a metal spoon over warm water and blend with the filling knife.
